coredata.dc:title: Boosting alkaline hydrogen oxidation by coupling of Ru nanoparticles with carbon dots

Wu, Yiru
Yang, Kunpeng
Yu, Jun
Zhang, Junhao
Zhu, Guoxing
coredata.dc:description: Reasonable design of efficient and environmentally friendly alkaline hydrogen oxidation (HOR) electrocatalysts is urgently required for the development of alkaline hydrogen-oxygen fuel cells. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were coupled with ruthenium (Ru) to form a hybrid Ru/CDs electrocatalyst. The combination of CDs with Ru nanoparticles can effectively inhibit the aggregation of Ru nanoparticles. Moreover, the electronic structure of Ru is modified due to the interaction between Ru and CDs. The optimized Ru/CDs catalyst exhibited excellent alkaline HOR activity with an exchange current density of 1.56 mA cm−2 that is significantly better than commercial Pt/C catalysts. These findings considerably broaden the potential applications of CDs and provide additional options for the development of high-performance HOR electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen oxidation
Carbon dots
Ru nanoparticles
Electrocatalysts
Hydrogen energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21776115), the open project supported by State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing (Wuhan University of Technology), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX24_4160), and the project supported by Key Laboratory of Advanced Electrode Materials for Novel Solar Cells for Petroleum and Chemical Industry of China (School of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Suzhou University of Science and Technology).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) XRD patterns of Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15 catalysts; TEM images of b–c) Ru/CDs-5 and d–e) Ru/CDs-15; f) HRTEM image of Ru/CDs-15; g–j) HADDF-STEM image and relevant element mapping of the Ru/CDs-5 catalyst. Insets of c) and e) are the corresponding size distribution.
a) FTIR spectra of Ru/CDs-5 and CDs. b) The Raman spectrum of Ru/CDs-5. c) XPS spectra of Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-0 samples. High-resolution XPS spectra for d) Ru 3p, e) C 1s, and f) O 1s.

a) Polarization curves collected on Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15, Ru/C, and Pt/C catalysts at 10 mV s−1 and the rotation rate of 1600 rpm. b) Polarization curves measured in KOH solution. c) Polarization curves of Ru/CDs-5 catalyst at different rotation rates. d) Koutecky-Levich plot of Ru/CDs-5 at an overpotential of 50 mV. e) HOR Tafel plots of j k for Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15, Ru/C, and Pt/C. f) Linear fitting curves from −5 to 5 mV for Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15, Ru/C, and Pt/C.

a) Polarization curves on Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15, Ru/CDs-0, Ru/C, and Pt/C catalysts with 1.0 M KOH at 5 mV s−1. b) Tafel slope of the prepared catalyst. c) The overpotential corresponding at 10 mA cm−2and 50 mA cm−2 of the prepared catalysts.
Durability test of Ru/CDs-5. a) CV and b) polarization curves for HOR. c) Polarization curves for HER.
Illustration for the synthesis of Ru/CDs.
Summary of the catalytic properties of the catalysts prepared in this work.

head.ce:title: Boosting alkaline hydrogen oxidation by coupling of Ru nanoparticles with carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reasonable design of efficient and environmentally friendly alkaline hydrogen oxidation (HOR) electrocatalysts is urgently required for the development of alkaline hydrogen-oxygen fuel cells. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were coupled with ruthenium (Ru) to form a hybrid Ru/CDs electrocatalyst. The combination of CDs with Ru nanoparticles can effectively inhibit the aggregation of Ru nanoparticles. Moreover, the electronic structure of Ru is modified due to the interaction between Ru and CDs. The optimized Ru/CDs catalyst exhibited excellent alkaline HOR activity with an exchange current density of 1.56 mA cm−2 that is significantly better than commercial Pt/C catalysts. These findings considerably broaden the potential applications of CDs and provide additional options for the development of high-performance HOR electrocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The swift consumption of fossil fuels has given rise to a succession of environmental and energy crises. Fuel cells represent a promising energy conversion technology that has garnered significant attention due to their exceptional efficiency, environmental sustainability, and zero carbon emissions [1,2]. Proton-exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) operating under acidic conditions require the utilization of platinum-based electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode. Challenges such as the sluggish kinetics of ORR and severe corrosion under acidic conditions significantly increase operational expenses and hinder the wide implementation of PEMFCs [3–5]. Compared to PEMFCs, anion exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) offer significant advantages, demonstrating excellent stability in alkaline conditions and a wider selection of cell components [6–8]. Based on current reports, non-precious metal catalysts have demonstrated comparable or potentially superior catalytic performance to platinum-based catalysts for ORR in alkaline system. Meanwhile, stable anion exchange membranes have been developed, further advancing the progress of alkaline fuel cells [9–11]. However, under alkaline conditions, the kinetics of the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) that takes place at the anode is 102-103 times lower than in the acidic environment [12,13]. This makes it imperative to develop highly efficient and stable non-platinum-based alkaline HOR catalysts.

For alkaline HOR, the critical active intermediators are adsorbed hydrogen (Had), hydroxyl (OHad) and adsorbed water (H2Oad) [14–17]. The mainstream theories for HOR include the hydrogen binding energy (HBE) theory and the bifunctional theory. HBE theory posits that the HBE of metal sites significantly influences the kinetics of the HOR, thus considering HBE as an activity descriptor. The bifunctional theory suggests that the adsorption of OH intermediates plays a crucial role, thereby introducing hydroxyl binding energy (OHBE) as an additional activity descriptor [18–20]. Ruthenium (Ru) is an ideal substitute for platinum (Pt) due to its similar hydrogen binding energy, the lower cost, and the notably higher tolerance for CO [21]. However, controlling the electronic structure of Ru catalytic sites to enhance its intrinsic activity remains a significant challenge.

Using the support to control the physical and chemical properties of the loaded metal is an effective method [22–24]. By altering the electronic structure of the supported metal through metal-support interactions, the adsorption strength of reaction intermediates can be controlled, thereby enhancing the catalytic performance for HOR [25–27]. Supporting Ru nanoparticles with a carbon-based substrate to form hybrid structures is an effective strategy for enhancing their activity and stability. However, the metal-support interaction between metal nanoparticles and carbon substrates with relatively inert surfaces is limited, thereby failing to meet the requirements for precise regulation of electronic structure [28,29]. Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional materials with an average size smaller than 10 nm. In comparison to carbon nanotube and graphene, carbon dots are distinguished by their straightforward production processes, cost-effectiveness, and environmental compatibility. They have been extensively utilized in environmental protection, energy storage, and biomedicine fields [30–33]. Furthermore, they also demonstrate distinctive properties including quantum confinement effects and various oxygen-containing functional groups [34–37]. The functional groups on carbon dots can undergo coordination reactions with metal ions, thereby enabling directed anchoring of metals, effectively inhibiting severe metal aggregation and promoting the formation of a highly active interface to facilitate electron transfer [38–40]. Currently, the application of CDs for HOR catalyst is limited. Therefore, the combination of Ru with carbon dots still holds significant research potential and academic value for the development of efficient and environmentally friendly HOR catalysts.

In this work, to investigate the influence of CDs on the catalytic performance of Ru in alkaline HOR, we synthesized a series of Ru/CDs electrocatalysts using CDs as the carrier. The carbon dots interconnected forming a matrix, in which Ru particles are loaded. Due to the presence of CDs, Ru particles with smaller size are formed, resulting in the exposure of amounts of active sites. The presence of abundant oxygen on CDs facilitates the rapid infiltration of electrolyte. The optimized catalytic performance is attained with a suitable amount of CDs, which exhibits a high exchange current density of 1.56 mA cm−2 and a kinetic current density of 3.52 mA cm−2 at 50 mV for alkaline HOR in KOH. The Ru/CDs catalyst also exhibits outstanding catalytic performance for HER with an extremely low overpotential of only 37 mV for 10 mA cm−2. This study highlights the significant potential of CDs as an innovative carbon material for hydrogen electrocatalysis, offering fresh opportunities for developing efficient hydrogen electrocatalysts.

Ruthenium (III) chloride anhydrous (RuCl3), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), ethylene glycol, cupric sulfate (CuSO4), ethanol (CH3CH2OH) and citric acid were all purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Co., Ltd. The Pt/C catalyst (20 wt% of Pt) is obtained from Yilongsheng energy Co., Ltd. Potassium hydroxide (KOH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%), were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Lt. Nafion (5 wt%) is purchased from Shanghai Branch, Du Pont China Holding Co., Lt. The chemicals were not subjected to any additional purification procedures. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was used throughout the whole experiment.

Typically, citric acid (1.05 g) and ethylenediamine (335 µL) was dissolved in 10 mL of water. The obtained mixture was transferred into a 20 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated at 200 °C for 16 h. After natural cooling, the product was subjected to dialysis and was freeze-dried for 12 h to get the solid CDs product.

Typically, 20 mg of RuCl3 and 5 mg of CDs was dispersed in 15 mL of ethylene glycol with the assistance of sonication. The thus obtained mixture was then added to a Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The formed solid sample was collected by centrifugation method and was washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times. The solid sample was then freeze-dried for 12 h. The products obtained with different feeding amounts of CDs (0, 5, and 15 mg) were designated as Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5, and Ru/CDs-15, respectively. For comparison, the contrast product of Ru/C was also synthesized under the same condition by replacing CDs with 5 mg of XC-72 carbon in the reaction system. Characterization of the samples are shown in the Supporting information (SI).

A classic three-electrode system was used for the test with Autolab PGSTAT204 workstation, a Hg/HgO (filled with 1.0 M KOH) electrode, a glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (GC-RDE) (disk area of 0.196 cm2), and a carbon rod as the reference, working, and counter electrodes, respectively. The preparation method of catalyst ink is described as follows: 2 mg of the catalyst and 8 mg of carbon black were dispersed into a mixture containing 480 μL of ethanol, 480 μL of isopropanol, and 40 μL of 5 wt% Nafion. The catalyst ink was sonicated for 3 h form a homogeneous catalyst ink.

Preparation of working electrodes: For HOR/HER tests, add 5 μL 14 μL of ink drops onto the GC-RDE surfaces and dries naturally in the air. (51 μg cm‐ 2 geo for HOR tests, 142 μg cm−2 geo for HER tests). The electrochemical HOR/HER testing is conducted in 0.1 M/1 M KOH solution. Prior to HOR/HER electrochemical measurements, the electrolyte is bubbled with H2/N2 for 30 min to reach its saturation. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed 20 times at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 to obtain a stable CV curve. Subsequently, the HOR polarization curve was obtained in a 0.1 M KOH electrolyte saturated with H2-saturated, employing four distinct scan rates of 400, 900, 1600, and 2500 rpm at a constant sweep rate of 10 mV s−1. HER polarization curve is obtained at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH. All tests were performed at room temperature. There is a corresponding relationship between the standard hydrogen electrode voltage and the voltage of the experimental three-electrode system by Eq. (1).
(1)
E =
E
Hg
/
HgO
+ 0.098 + 0.059 * pH

Electrochemically surface areas (ECSA) were tested by Cu underpotential deposition (CuUPD) stripping experiment. Typically, CV test on each catalyst was firstly carried out with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in N2-purged 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with 0.035–0.70 V (vs Hg/Hg2SO4) to obtain a steady voltammogram curve as the background. Then, CuUPD deposition-stripping was performed at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in the same solution containing 2 mM CuSO4 with Cu deposition at about 0.30 V (vs Hg/Hg2SO4) for 150 s. The ECSA value can be calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
ECSA =
Q
Cu
Q
s
m
metal
where QCu is the measured charge integral, Qs is surface charge density of 420 μC cmmetal −2, and mmetal is the total amount of metallic ruthenium on the electrode.

The reaction between ethylenediamine and citric acid is used to prepare CDs (Fig. S1, see SI). In a typical synthesis process for the synthesis of Ru/CDs, CDs and RuCl3 are dispersed in ethylene glycol with CDs acting as structure-directed agents, while ethylene glycol serves as a reducing agent for Ru (III) species ( Scheme 1). The prepared carbon dots own amounts of functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces, which can coordinate and anchor metal ions. During the solvothermal reaction, these functional groups on the CDs surface can coordinate with Ru3+, stably anchoring it on the CDs surface and effectively inhibiting the excessive growth of Ru nanoparticles. During the reaction process, further dehydration reaction will occur between CDs, which will induce the growth, ultimately promoting the transformation from a zero-dimensional structure to a two or three-dimensional carbon structure (Scheme 1).

The catalysts were firstly monitored by X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. As exhibited in Fig. 1a, Ru/CDs-0 showed diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38.5, 42.3, 44.0, 58.5, 69.3, and 78.1°, which can be indexed into the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), and (103) planes of hexagonal Ru (PDF#97-005-4236). The products with CDs (Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15) show a wide peak at 2θ = 42.3°, which is caused by the smaller size of Ru particles (PDF#97-005-4236). When the CDs dosage was further increased (the sample of Ru/CDs-15), an additional peak related to carbon was observed at 22.4° [41]. The Ru contents in Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-15 products are 8.64 wt% and 7.16 wt%, respectively, as determined by ICP-OES analysis (Table S1. see SI).

The microscale morphology of the typical product (Ru/CDs-5) was then analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images of Ru/CDs-5 are shown in Fig. 1b-c. Ru nanoparticles are homogeneously loaded in the carbon matrix formed by CDs. This can be clearly seen from the HAADF-STEM images (Fig. S2, see SI). The size distribution of Ru particles in the product of Ru/CDs-5 indicates an average particle size of 2.0 nm. As shown in Fig. S3, the Ru/CDs-0 product of shows bigger units with agglomerated and irregular particles. The presence of CDs effectively suppresses the excessive growth of Ru, thereby preventing the occurrence of aggregation and facilitating the formation of smaller and well dispersed ruthenium particles. The Ru/CDs-15 product shows a quite similar structure as that of Ru/CDs-5 (Fig. 1d–e). The Ru particles show size of about 2.2 nm that is close to the Ru/CDs-5. In contrast, the product obtained without CDs shows quite different microstructure. Fig. 1f show the high resolution TEM image. The fringes with spacing of 0.205 nm was observed, which can be ascribed to the (101) crystal plane of Ru. The region with lattice fringes of 0.21 nm can be attributed to the (100) planar spacing of carbon. The HAADF-STEM and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) element mapping images reveal a homogeneous distribution of the Ru (Fig. 1g–j, Fig. S3, and Fig S4, see SI). Only a very weak signal was found for carbon, which may be caused by the carbon film background. Additionally, a small amount of oxygen element can also be observed. This is caused by the presence of organic oxygen-containing functional groups on carbon dots and the inevitable occurrence of partial oxidation on the surface of Ru [42].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to analyze the characteristic functional groups of the CDs and Ru/CDs products ( Fig. 2a). Obviously, the series of characteristic peaks, such as O-H (3401 cm−1), C-H (2919 cm−1), CO (1661–1592 cm−1), and C-O (1406–1316 cm−1) functional groups, were observed. These bands indicate the presence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs [43]. In the UV-Vis spectrum, the peak was observed at 345 nm for the dispersion of CDs (Fig. S1b, see SI). Infrared spectra of CDs and Ru/CDs-5 exhibit remarkable consistency in characteristic peaks, suggesting that Ru/CDs inherits the functional groups present on CDs. These functional groups not only anchor Ru ions and mediate the growth of Ru particles for the preparation of Ru/CDs composites, but also facilitate the adsorption of water molecules via the formation of abundant hydrogen bonds, thereby helping the rapid transfer of protons during the catalytic process [44,45]. The Raman spectra are shown in Fig. 2b, the D-band (at 1347 cm−1) and G-band (at 1569 cm−1) of carbon with I D /I G intensity ratio of 1.09 are observed for Ru/CDs-5. This is higher than that for CDs with I D /I G
of 0.51 (Fig. S1c, see SI), indicating that the Ru/CDs product possesses disordered carbon [46].

The elemental composition and surface chemical state of Ru/CDs-0 and Ru/CDs-5 were then studied in detail by XPS (Fig. 2c–f). It was revealed that the Ru/CD surface is primarily composed of Ru, C, and O elements. Fig. 2d shows the XPS spectrum for Ru 3p; the peaks at 484.8 and 462.4 eV corresponded to Ru 3p1/2 and 3p3/2 peaks of Ru0; while the bands at 487.4 and 464.2 eV corresponded to Ru4+ respectively [47,48], unveiling that the Ru species in the catalysts exist mainly as metallic Ru. In the presence of CDs, the Ru 3p XPS peaks present a blue-shift if compared with that of Ru/CDs-0, implying the electron transfer from Ru to CDs, which would reduce the electron density on Ru sites. The peaks at 284.8, 286.0, and 288. eV can be assigned to CC, C-O and CO groups (Fig. 2e) [49]. The O1s XPS peak is mainly composed of three parts: one at 530.4 eV attributing to Ru-O group, one at 532.2 eV attributing to C-OH and CO groups, and another at 533.7 eV corresponding to O-CO units (Fig. 2f) [50]. It seems that the relative content of Ru-O increased with the addition of CDs. This may be caused by the higher oxygen content in CDs. It has been documented that the presence of suitable oxides can modulate the electronic structure of the catalyst centers, optimize OH– adsorption, and consequently enhance the catalytic activity for HOR [51].

The electrocatalytic HOR activity of Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5, Ru/CDs-15, commercial Pt/C, and Ru/C catalysts was systematically tested in a H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. The HOR polarization curve at an electrode speed of 1600 rpm and a scanning rate of 10 mV s−1 is depicted in Fig. 3a. The current density exhibits a consistent upward trend with the increase in potential range within 0–0.15 V (vs. RHE). The Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-15 catalysts exhibited current density of 2.39 and 1.77 mA cm−2 at 50 mV (vs RHE), respectively. The current density is higher than that of the Ru/CDs-0 (0.68 mA cm−2), Ru/C (1.46 mA cm−2) and Pt/C (1.62 mA cm−2) catalysts. At a higher potential (>0.13 V vs. RHE), the current density gradually decreases. This is caused by the fact that the Ru surface is inevitably occupied by in situ formed oxides, inducing a decrease of the active site amount and so a decrease in the current density [52,53]. By comparing the performance of Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-15, it can be clearly seen that the introduction of CDs significantly enhances the catalytic performance of Ru. Further comparison of the results of Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-15 indicates that the catalytic activity is not linearly and positively correlated with the amount of CDs added, but shows a volcano-shaped trend with an optimal value. The reason for the decline in the catalytic performance of Ru/CDs-15 may be that the more CDs covers some of the active sites of ruthenium, thereby reducing the catalytic performance. Furthermore, the performance comparison with Ru/C clearly highlights the unique advantages of CDs as exceptional carbon support. The polarization curves obtained in a 0.1 M KOH solution saturated with H2 and N2 are compared in Fig. 3b. The catalyst exhibits a very weak anodic current in the N2-saturated electrolyte, indicating that the anodic current observed in a H2-saturated environment mainly originates from the oxidation reaction of hydrogen. In N2-saturated electrolyte solution, a weak oxidation peak can also be observed, which may be attributed to the catalyst surface adsorbing trace amounts of hydrogen and undergoing an oxidation reaction [54].

The reaction kinetics of Ru/CDs-5 were investigated by acquiring polarization curves under H2-saturation electrolyte at various rotation rates from 400 to 2500 rpm. The results presented in Fig. 3c illustrate a clear correlation between the rotation speed and the increase in current density, which can be attributed to the acceleration of the transfer of active substances due to the increase of rotational speed. Fig. 3d shows the Koutecky-Levich plot with the current densities at 50 mV for Ru/CDs-5, demonstrating a linear relationship between the total current density and the inverse ratio of the square root of the rotation rate [50,55,56] (Details for the calculation is shown in Table S2). This analysis yields a calculated slope of 4.07 cm2 mA−1 s−1/2 for Ru/CDs-5. This obtained value approximates 4.87 cm2 mA−1 s−1/2 that is the theoretical value for HOR with two-electron transfer [57]. The contrast samples of Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-15, Ru/C and commercial Pt/C present slopes in the range of 3.52–4.46 cm2 mA−1 s−1/2 (Figs. S5-8, see SI).

The kinetic current densities (j k) were analyzed according to the Koutecky-Levich equation (Eq. (3)).
(3)
1 j
=
1
j k
+
1
j d
=
1
j k
+
1
Bc 0
ω
1 2
where j, j d, j k, B, c 0 and ω represent the, tested current density, diffusion limited current density, kinetic current density, Levich constant (Eq. (S1), see SI), solubility of H2 in 0.1 M KOH, rotating speed, respectively. The Tafel plots further support the highest activity of the Ru/CDs-5 catalyst (Fig. 3e). The product of Ru/CDs-5 shows the largest kinetic current density derived from the Koutecky-Levich equation. The j k value at 50 mV for Ru/CDs-5 is estimated to be 3.52 mA cm−2. This is 1.5 times of Pt/C (2.06 mA cm−2) and also significantly higher than other catalysts (0.59–2.60 mA cm−2) ( Table 1). In addition, the kinetic current densities were also normalized by the Ru content. The Ru/CDs-5 catalyst provides a normalized kinetic current density of 0.819 mA μg−1 at potential of 50 mV, which is higher most of reported HOR catalysts, suggesting the excellent catalytic activity (Table S2, see SI).

The exchange current density (j 0) serves as a crucial parameter for characterizing the catalytic activity. The j 0 value of the catalyst can be obtained by the fitting with the Butler-Volmer equation (Eq. (4)) in the micro-polarization region (−5 to 5 mV) (Fig. 3f, Eq. (5)).
(4)
j
k
=
j
0
[
e
α F
RT
η
−
e
−
1 − α
F
RT
η
]
(5)
j 0
=
jRT ηF
where R, T, F, η, α, and j 0 represent the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), the temperature in Kelvin (298 K), Faraday’s constant (96,485 C mol−1), overpotential, the transfer coefficient and the exchange current density. The calculated j 0 values for the Ru/CDs-5 catalyst is 1.54 mA cm−2, which surpasses that of Ru/CDs-15 (1.20 mA cm−2), Ru/CDs-0 (0.44 mA cm−2), Ru/C (1.10 mA cm−2), and the Pt/C catalyst (0.96 mA cm−2) (Table S3, see SI).

The above results confirmed that the catalytic performance of Ru particles for HOR can be enhanced with the incorporation of CDs. In order to consider the influence of the ECSA value on the catalytic performance, the ECSA values were checked by Cu-UPD stripping voltammograms (Fig. S9, see SI). The normalized exchange current density (j 0 , ECSA) was then estimated, which can more truly and intuitively reflect the intrinsic performance of catalysts (Fig. S10, see SI). In Table 1 and Fig. S10, the Ru/CDs-5 catalyst exhibits the highest normalized exchange current density of 0.93 mA cm−2, which further proves its excellent catalytic performance and the positive effect of CDs on the catalyst performance. The performance of the reported catalysts in HOR was compared, and the results manifestly indicated that Ru/CDs-5 exhibited outstanding catalytic activity (Table S3, see SI).

In this work, we also conducted performance tests on the obtained catalyst to evaluate its catalytic activity towards hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The HER electrochemical activity of each catalytic material was measured and analyzed in N2-saturated KOH electrolyte using a standard three-electrode system. Fig. 4a shows the catalytic performance of Ru/CDs-5, which surpasses that of commercial Pt/C catalyst, exhibiting an overpotential of only 37 mV for current density of 10 mA cm−2. Notably, as depicted in the Fig. 4b, the Tafel slope is 45.6 mV dec−1 for Ru/CDs-5. This is significantly smaller than commercial Pt/C catalyst (92.7 mV dec−1). These experimental results demonstrate that the Ru/CDs-5 catalyst also exhibits the most outstanding excellent performance in HER, which is consistent with its performance in HOR. Furthermore, the Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-15 products exhibit lower overpotential and Tafel slopes if compared to the other catalysts (Table S5, see SI). This further demonstrates the crucial role of CDs as a new carbon material for the enhancing of the catalytic performance of Ru sites. Fig. 4c presents the comparison of the overpotential values for the samples at 10 and 50 mA cm−2, revealing that the Ru/CDs-5 product demonstrates superior catalytic activity among the catalysts investigated. Furthermore, when compared to previously reported ruthenium-based catalysts, the optimized catalyst exhibits outstanding performance in HER (Table S6, see SI).

The durability is a key parameter to evaluate the excellent performance of catalysts. The durability of Ru/CDs-5 was evaluated through accelerated durability testing. CV and LSV curves of the catalyst were recorded before and after 2000 CV cycles between −0.1 and 0.4 V (vs. RHE) for comparison ( Fig. 5a−b). It was found that the catalyst exhibits remarkable stability, only demonstrating a 12 % (from 3.57 to 3.12 mA cm−2) of decrease in the current density after 2000 CV cycles, while the exchange current density only experiences a small decline of 6 % (from 1.54 to 1.44 mA cm−2). Fig. 5c shows the LSV curves before and after 2000 CV iterations for HER, revealing a marginal decline in the catalytic current density. The overpotential for HER slightly changed, increasing by 8 mV for 10 mA cm−2(from 37 to 45 mV)

According to the widely accepted viewpoint, HOR mainly undergoes the following three element steps under alkaline condition. The Tafel or Heyrovsky step involves the formation of Had intermediates. The Volmer step involves the formation of water.
(6) Tafel step: H2 + 2 M* → 2Had-M
(7) Heyrovsky step: H2 + OH− + M* → Had-M + H2O + e−
(8) Volmer step: Had-M + OH− → H2O + e− + M*

M* presents the metal centers. During the reaction, the critical steps are the adsorption and desorption of H+ and OH-. Therefore, optimizing of HBE, OHBE, and the involved mass transfer efficiency is critical for the HOR process. For our synthesized Ru/CDs-5 catalyst, CDs are interconnected forming a matrix, on which Ru NPs are loaded. CDs possess characteristics of both electron donors and acceptors, promoting enhanced electron transfer and transport efficiency for our catalysis process [58,59]. In our case, the introduction of CDs causes a positive shift in the Ru 3p band of Ru/CDs-5 relative to that of Ru/CDs-0, indicating that CDs alter the electronic structure of Ru, causing electrons transporting from Ru to CDs. This modification in electronic structure would induce the decrease of hydrogen binding and the increase of OH binding on the Ru sites. The HBE on Ru sites of Ru/CDs-0, Ru/CDs-5 and Ru/CDs-15 was then investigated by HUPD stripping peak. The results are consistent with expectations. Ru/CDs-5 showed a more negative HUPD stripping peak, indicating that it had the most suitable HBE (Fig. S11, see SI). This result proved that the introduction of CDs successfully optimized the HBE on Ru sites [60]. In addition, the surface of CDs own amounts of hydrophilic functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which may facilitate the rapid infiltration of electrolyte. This will also no doubt enhance the efficiency of mass transfer between the electrolyte and the catalyst surface, and thus inducing an enhanced catalytic performance.

In summary, a series of Ru/CDs catalysts with varied carbon content were synthesized, in which CDs forms a matrix; Ru particles are uniformly loaded on them with smaller size. The close contact between CDs and the Ru particles facilitates the metal-support interaction, thereby modifying the electronic structure of Ru, which optimizes the HBE and OHBE. The characteristics of the CDs as both of electron donors and acceptors also promote the hydrophilic surface to facilitate the rapid infiltration of electrolyte. The limit current density of the optimized Ru/CDs-5 catalyst reached 3.57 mA cm−2, while its exchange current density was 1.5 times that of commercial Pt/C. The same catalyst, Ru/CDs-5, also demonstrated only a 37 mV of overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 for HER. In addition, the Ru/CDs-5 catalyst also presents a long-term durability. This study would provide some useful hints for the development of advanced hydrogen catalysts.

Yiru Wu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuanjun Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Guoxing Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Junhao Zhang: Resources, Formal analysis. Jun Yu: Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Kunpeng Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21776115), the open project supported by State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing (Wuhan University of Technology), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX24_4160), and the project supported by Key Laboratory of Advanced Electrode Materials for Novel Solar Cells for Petroleum and Chemical Industry of China (School of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Suzhou University of Science and Technology).
